---
title: "Ordinary Least Squares, One Predictor"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Regression"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT = "#2b3a67"     # data points
LINE  = "#c1121f"     # fitted line
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights / means
GREY  = "#6c757d"

def ols_fit(x, y):
    """Return (b0, b1, Sxx, Sxy) for simple OLS — the very formulas this
    article derives, used here only to draw the pictures."""
    n = len(x)
    xbar, ybar = x.mean(), y.mean()
    Sxx = np.sum((x - xbar) ** 2)
    Sxy = np.sum((x - xbar) * (y - ybar))
    b1 = Sxy / Sxx
    b0 = ybar - b1 * xbar
    return b0, b1, Sxx, Sxy
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the true entry point of the Regression
> track. It assumes only [REGR-0 *Why a Line?*], which established *what* regression is
> after — estimating how the average of an outcome moves with a predictor — and *why*
> "draw the best line through the cloud" is meaningless until we pin down the word
> *best*. Here we make that commitment (squared error) and follow it all the way to a
> closed-form line. It sets up [REGR-2 *What the Line Assumes*] (when this line is
> trustworthy), [REGR-3 *How Good Is the Fit?*] (how we score it), and [REGR-4
> *Regression as Projection*] (the same answer seen as geometry).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Given paired data $(x_1,y_1),\dots,(x_n,y_n)$ and the decision to measure error by the
*sum of squared vertical distances*, exactly one straight line is best. This article
derives it from nothing but Calculus I. We write down the error as a function of the
intercept and slope, $S(\beta_0,\beta_1)$, differentiate it, set the derivatives to
zero, and solve. Two clean formulas fall out:
$$\hat\beta_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \qquad \hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x.$$
The single claim this article defends is that **these are not a recipe to memorize but
the forced consequence of one choice** — squared error — and that reading the slope as
*covariance over variance* tells you when the line will serve you and when it will lie.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Commit to measuring a line's error by the **sum of squared vertical gaps** between the
data and the line. Then the best line is no longer a matter of taste: calculus pins down
exactly one. Its slope is the **covariance of $x$ and $y$ divided by the variance of
$x$**, and it always passes through the point of averages $(\bar x,\bar y)$.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The slope formula $\hat\beta_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}$ will
appear to drop out of grinding algebra, but by the end you should see it as inevitable:
*how much $y$ and $x$ move together, scaled by how much $x$ moves at all.* If they move
together strongly relative to $x$'s own spread, the line is steep; if $x$ barely varies,
the slope is unstable — a fact that will return as a genuine failure mode in
@sec-failure.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the line {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why squared error, and why does it
hand us a line we can actually compute?** Every algebraic step below is an answer to some
piece of that question. If you ever feel a step is arbitrary, return here.

## The original problem

The setting that birthed least squares was not statistics class — it was astronomy and
geodesy around 1800. Astronomers had many noisy observations of a planet's position and
wanted a single best orbit. Surveyors measuring the shape of the Earth had more
measurements than unknowns and no two agreed exactly. They faced an **over-determined**
problem: more equations than unknowns, no exact solution, and a need to *reconcile*
conflicting measurements into one answer.

Adrien-Marie Legendre published the method of least squares in 1805 as an appendix on
cometary orbits; Carl Friedrich Gauss claimed (and used) it earlier and tied it to the
normal distribution of errors. Their shared move is the one we inherit: **declare that
the best reconciliation is the one minimizing the total squared discrepancy**, then solve
for it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why squared error? Why not the sum of absolute errors, or the maximum error?"* Three
honest reasons, and you should be able to give all three.

1. **It is differentiable everywhere.** $|e|$ has a corner at $e=0$; $e^2$ is smooth, so
   we can use calculus and get a clean closed-form answer — which is the whole point of
   this article. Absolute error gives a problem with no formula, solvable only
   numerically (that line is the *median* regression, a real and useful alternative).
2. **It has a clean statistical meaning.** Minimizing squared error coincides with
   maximum likelihood when the noise is Gaussian — derived in full in [REGR-12].
3. **It penalizes big misses hard.** Squaring weights a gap of 4 sixteen times a gap of
   1, so the line works hard to avoid large errors.

And the honest catch you must volunteer: reason 3 is *also* its great weakness. Squared
error is hypersensitive to outliers, because one far-off point contributes an enormous
squared term and can drag the whole line toward itself. We will see exactly this in
@sec-failure. The interviewer is testing whether you know the cost of the choice, not
just the choice.
:::

## What we are committing to

We are given $n$ pairs $(x_i, y_i)$. We propose to summarize them with a straight line,

$$
\hat y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x,
$$ {#eq-line}

where $\beta_0$ is the **intercept** (the line's height at $x=0$) and $\beta_1$ is the
**slope** (the change in $\hat y$ per unit change in $x$). The hat on $\hat y$ marks it
as a *prediction* from the line, distinct from the observed $y_i$.

For a candidate line with parameters $(\beta_0,\beta_1)$, the line predicts
$\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$ at the $i$-th point, while we actually observed $y_i$. The
**residual** is the vertical gap

$$
e_i = y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i),
$$ {#eq-resid}

the signed distance from the data point *up to* the line (positive when the point sits
above the line). @fig-residuals-anatomy shows precisely these gaps. Our chosen score for
a whole line is the sum of the squares of these gaps.

```{python}
#| label: fig-residuals-anatomy
#| fig-cap: "The anatomy of least squares. Each vertical red segment is a residual $e_i = y_i - (\\beta_0+\\beta_1 x_i)$ — the gap we square and add up. OLS chooses the line that makes the total squared length of these segments as small as possible. Note the residuals are *vertical*, not perpendicular: we are predicting $y$ from $x$, so only errors in $y$ count."
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
x = np.linspace(1, 9, 9)
y = 2.0 + 0.8 * x + rng.normal(0, 1.1, size=x.size)
b0, b1, *_ = ols_fit(x, y)
xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color=LINE, label=fr"fitted line $\hat y = {b0:.2f} + {b1:.2f}x$")
for xi, yi in zip(x, y):
    ax.plot([xi, xi], [yi, b0 + b1 * xi], color=GREY, lw=1.4, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(x, y, color=POINT, zorder=3, s=45, label="data $(x_i, y_i)$")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title("Residuals are the vertical gaps we square")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any calculus, picture the search. We have a flat cloud of points. We slide a rigid
straight line around — tilting it (changing $\beta_1$) and raising or lowering it
(changing $\beta_0$) — and for each position we measure a single number: the total
squared vertical gap. We want the position where that number bottoms out.

Here is the key mental image that makes the calculus unsurprising. **The score is a bowl.**
As a function of the two knobs $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$, the total squared error
$S(\beta_0,\beta_1)$ is a smooth, upward-opening bowl (a paraboloid) — it has exactly one
lowest point, and at that lowest point the surface is flat in every direction. "Flat in
every direction" is precisely what "both partial derivatives equal zero" means. So the
entire derivation in @sec-formal is just: *find the bottom of the bowl by finding where
it is flat.* @fig-bowl draws the bowl.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bowl
#| fig-cap: "The error surface $S(\\beta_0,\\beta_1)$ is a single convex bowl over the plane of candidate lines. Every choice of intercept and slope is a point on the floor; its height is the total squared error of that line. There is exactly one lowest point (marked), and the surface is flat there in both directions. The derivation that follows is nothing more than solving for that flat spot."
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

xs_d = np.linspace(1, 9, 9)
ys_d = 2.0 + 0.8 * xs_d + np.random.default_rng(7).normal(0, 1.1, size=xs_d.size)

b0g = np.linspace(-2, 6, 60)
b1g = np.linspace(-0.6, 2.2, 60)
B0, B1 = np.meshgrid(b0g, b1g)
S = np.zeros_like(B0)
for i in range(B0.shape[0]):
    for j in range(B0.shape[1]):
        pred = B0[i, j] + B1[i, j] * xs_d
        S[i, j] = np.sum((ys_d - pred) ** 2)

b0_star, b1_star, *_ = ols_fit(xs_d, ys_d)
S_star = np.sum((ys_d - (b0_star + b1_star * xs_d)) ** 2)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.4, 4.6))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(B0, B1, S, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.85, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
ax.scatter([b0_star], [b1_star], [S_star], color=LINE, s=60, depthshade=False)
ax.text(b0_star, b1_star, S_star + 30, "minimum", color=LINE)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_0$ (intercept)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_1$ (slope)")
ax.set_zlabel(r"$S(\beta_0,\beta_1)$")
ax.set_title("The squared-error surface is one convex bowl")
ax.view_init(elev=28, azim=-128)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How do you know setting the derivatives to zero gives a minimum and not a maximum or a
saddle?"* Because $S$ is a sum of squared terms — it is bounded below by $0$ and grows
without bound as the line runs away from the data, so its one stationary point must be
the minimum. We confirm this rigorously in @sec-second-order using the second
derivatives, exactly the Calc-1 second-derivative test. The bowl picture is the intuition;
the second-order check is the proof.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The objective function {#sec-objective}

::: {#def-objective}
**Definition (least-squares objective).** For data $(x_i,y_i)_{i=1}^n$, the **sum of
squared errors** as a function of the candidate intercept $\beta_0$ and slope $\beta_1$ is

$$
S(\beta_0,\beta_1) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr)^2 .
$$ {#eq-objective}
:::

Read @eq-objective slowly, because every symbol earns its place. The sum $\sum_{i=1}^n$
runs over all $n$ data points. Inside, $y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i$ is the residual from
@eq-resid (the gap), and the square makes every gap a positive contribution that grows
quadratically. The data $(x_i,y_i)$ are *fixed, known numbers*; the *variables* of this
function are $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. That last sentence is the entire conceptual pivot of
the article, so we dwell on it.

::: {.callout-important title="The pivot: what is variable and what is fixed"}
In high-school algebra, $x$ is the variable and the slope/intercept are fixed numbers
describing a known line. **Here it is the reverse.** The data $x_i, y_i$ are the fixed,
already-collected numbers; the unknowns we are solving *for* are $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.
We are not asking "what is $y$ at a given $x$"; we are asking "which line —which
$(\beta_0,\beta_1)$— is best." Holding this straight is what makes the partial
derivatives below make sense.
:::

## A two-minute refresher on the partial derivative {#sec-partial-refresher}

Our objective $S$ depends on *two* variables, $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. Minimizing a
function of two variables is technically multivariable calculus, which sits above our
Calculus-I floor — so we build the one tool we need from scratch, and we need only its
Calc-1 shadow.

A **partial derivative** of a two-variable function is computed by an almost insultingly
simple rule: **to differentiate with respect to one variable, treat the other variable as
if it were a constant number, then differentiate normally with the ordinary Calc-1 rules
you already know.** The symbol is $\partial$ instead of $d$, purely to signal "there are
other variables hanging around that I am holding still." That is the whole idea.

A worked micro-example so there is zero mystery. Let $g(a,b) = (5 - a - 3b)^2$. To get
$\partial g/\partial a$, freeze $b$ (pretend $3b$ is just some number) and use the chain
rule from Calc 1, $\frac{d}{du}[u^2] = 2u\cdot u'$:

$$
\frac{\partial g}{\partial a}
= 2\,(5 - a - 3b)\cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial a}(5 - a - 3b)}_{=\,-1}
= -2\,(5 - a - 3b).
$$

The inner derivative is $-1$ because, treating $b$ as constant, $5$ and $-3b$ are
constants (derivative $0$) and $-a$ contributes $-1$. The minimization below is *only*
this move, applied to @eq-objective, twice. Every term we will differentiate has the
shape $(\text{stuff})^2$, so the chain rule $2(\text{stuff})\times(\text{derivative of
stuff})$ is the engine throughout.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why partial derivatives and not just one ordinary derivative?"* Because the height of
the bowl depends on two independent knobs. The minimum requires the surface to be flat
along the $\beta_0$ axis **and** flat along the $\beta_1$ axis simultaneously — two
conditions, hence two partial derivatives, hence (as we will see) two equations in two
unknowns.
:::

## Differentiating with respect to the intercept {#sec-dS-db0}

We find where the bowl is flat in the $\beta_0$ direction. Start from @eq-objective and
differentiate term by term — the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_0}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr)^2
= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_0}\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr)^2 .
$$

Now apply the chain rule to one term. The "stuff" is $u_i = y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i$.
Treating $\beta_1$ (and the data) as constant, the derivative of $u_i$ with respect to
$\beta_0$ is $-1$ (only the $-\beta_0$ term survives). So each term gives

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_0}\,u_i^2
= 2u_i \cdot \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial \beta_0}
= 2\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr)\cdot(-1)
= -2\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr).
$$

Summing over $i$ and pulling the constant $-2$ out front:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0}
= -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr).
$$ {#eq-dSdb0}

## Differentiating with respect to the slope {#sec-dS-db1}

Same move, now freezing $\beta_0$ and differentiating with respect to $\beta_1$. The only
change is the inner derivative: with $\beta_0$ held constant,
$\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta_1}\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr) = -x_i$ (the
$-\beta_1 x_i$ term contributes $-x_i$; everything else is constant). Then

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_1}\,u_i^2
= 2u_i\cdot\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial \beta_1}
= 2\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr)\cdot(-x_i)
= -2x_i\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr),
$$

and summing,

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1}
= -2\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\bigr).
$$ {#eq-dSdb1}

The single difference between @eq-dSdb0 and @eq-dSdb1 is the extra factor of $x_i$ inside
the sum — the fingerprint of the chain rule's inner derivative ($-1$ versus $-x_i$). Keep
an eye on that $x_i$; it is what makes the two equations carry different information.

## Setting both to zero: the normal equations {#sec-normal-eqs}

At the bottom of the bowl, both partial derivatives vanish. Set @eq-dSdb0 and @eq-dSdb1
to zero. We write the minimizing values with hats — $\hat\beta_0, \hat\beta_1$ — to mark
them as *the solution*, no longer free variables:

$$
-2\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - \hat\beta_0 - \hat\beta_1 x_i\bigr) = 0,
\qquad
-2\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\bigl(y_i - \hat\beta_0 - \hat\beta_1 x_i\bigr) = 0.
$$

Divide both by $-2$ (a nonzero constant, so it cannot change where the zero is) and
distribute the sums over their three terms. Recall that $\sum \hat\beta_0 = n\hat\beta_0$
because $\hat\beta_0$ is the same number added $n$ times, and constants pull out of sums.
From the first equation:

$$
\sum y_i - \sum \hat\beta_0 - \sum \hat\beta_1 x_i = 0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\sum_{i=1}^n y_i - n\hat\beta_0 - \hat\beta_1\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = 0.
$$

From the second equation, distributing the $x_i$ inside first
($x_i\cdot\hat\beta_0 = \hat\beta_0 x_i$ and $x_i\cdot\hat\beta_1 x_i = \hat\beta_1 x_i^2$):

$$
\sum x_i y_i - \hat\beta_0\sum x_i - \hat\beta_1\sum x_i^2 = 0.
$$

Rearranging each so the unknowns sit on the left, we obtain the celebrated **normal
equations** — two linear equations in the two unknowns $\hat\beta_0, \hat\beta_1$:

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
n\,\hat\beta_0 \;+\; \Bigl(\textstyle\sum x_i\Bigr)\hat\beta_1 &= \textstyle\sum y_i, \\[4pt]
\Bigl(\textstyle\sum x_i\Bigr)\hat\beta_0 \;+\; \Bigl(\textstyle\sum x_i^2\Bigr)\hat\beta_1 &= \textstyle\sum x_i y_i.
\end{aligned}
\;}
$$ {#eq-normal}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why are they called the 'normal' equations?"* Not because they are ordinary. "Normal"
here means *perpendicular*. The first normal equation says $\sum e_i = 0$ and the second
says $\sum x_i e_i = 0$ — i.e. the residual vector is **orthogonal** to both the constant
vector and the $x$ vector. That orthogonality is the entire geometric story of least
squares, taken up in full in [REGR-4 *Regression as Projection*]. For now, just register
that "normal" = "at right angles," and that we have *two* equations because we set *two*
partials to zero.
:::

## Solving the system {#sec-solve}

We now solve @eq-normal. There are two clean routes; we take the one that reveals the
meaning. **Solve the first equation for $\hat\beta_0$, then substitute.**

Divide the first normal equation by $n$:

$$
\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1\,\frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n}.
$$

The two averages are exactly the sample means
$\bar x = \frac1n\sum x_i$ and $\bar y = \frac1n\sum y_i$, so this reads
$\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1\bar x = \bar y$, which rearranges to

$$
\boxed{\;\hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1\,\bar x.\;}
$$ {#eq-b0}

That is the intercept formula already — but it still contains $\hat\beta_1$, so we need
the slope. Notice immediately what @eq-b0 says geometrically: rearranged, it is
$\bar y = \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1\bar x$, i.e. **the fitted line passes through the
point of averages $(\bar x, \bar y)$.** We will use and re-derive that fact in
@sec-through-means.

Now substitute @eq-b0 into the second normal equation to eliminate $\hat\beta_0$. The
second equation is $\hat\beta_0\sum x_i + \hat\beta_1\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$. Replace
$\hat\beta_0$ with $\bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x$:

$$
(\bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x)\sum x_i \;+\; \hat\beta_1\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i.
$$

Distribute the $\sum x_i$ across the parenthesis:

$$
\bar y\sum x_i - \hat\beta_1\bar x\sum x_i + \hat\beta_1\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i.
$$

Collect the two $\hat\beta_1$ terms on the left and move the lone $\bar y\sum x_i$ to the
right:

$$
\hat\beta_1\Bigl(\sum x_i^2 - \bar x\sum x_i\Bigr) = \sum x_i y_i - \bar y\sum x_i.
$$

Therefore, dividing by the parenthesized factor,

$$
\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\sum x_i y_i - \bar y\sum x_i}{\sum x_i^2 - \bar x\sum x_i}.
$$ {#eq-b1-raw}

This is correct but ugly. We now show it equals the clean covariance/variance form
promised in the Core. Two small identities do all the work, and we prove both rather than
assert them.

## Two bookkeeping identities (proved, not assumed) {#sec-identities}

::: {#lem-identities}
**Lemma (centering identities).** With $\bar x = \frac1n\sum x_i$ and
$\bar y = \frac1n\sum y_i$,

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y) = \sum x_i y_i - \bar y\sum x_i,
\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\bar x)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \bar x\sum x_i.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the first; the second is the special case $y\equiv x$. Expand the product inside
the sum using ordinary FOIL:

$$
(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y) = x_i y_i - x_i\bar y - \bar x\, y_i + \bar x\,\bar y .
$$

Sum each of the four pieces over $i$, pulling the constants $\bar x,\bar y$ outside their
sums:

$$
\sum (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)
= \sum x_i y_i \;-\; \bar y\sum x_i \;-\; \bar x\sum y_i \;+\; n\,\bar x\,\bar y .
$$

The last term has $n$ because $\bar x\bar y$ is a constant summed $n$ times. Now use the
defining fact $\sum y_i = n\bar y$ (and likewise $\sum x_i = n\bar x$) on the third and
fourth terms:

$$
-\,\bar x\sum y_i + n\bar x\bar y
= -\,\bar x\,(n\bar y) + n\bar x\bar y
= -\,n\bar x\bar y + n\bar x\bar y = 0 .
$$

Those two terms cancel exactly, leaving

$$
\sum (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y) = \sum x_i y_i - \bar y\sum x_i ,
$$

which is the first identity. Setting $y_i = x_i$ throughout gives the second. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

These identities are pure algebra — no statistics, no assumptions about the data. They
matter because the left-hand sides are *centered* quantities with a clean interpretation,
while the right-hand sides are exactly the messy numerators and denominators in
@eq-b1-raw.

## The clean slope, and its meaning {#sec-clean-slope}

Define the two **sums of squares and cross-products** — the standard shorthand of
regression:

$$
S_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\bar x)^2,
\qquad
S_{xy} = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y).
$$ {#eq-Sxx-Sxy}

By @lem-identities, the numerator of @eq-b1-raw is exactly $S_{xy}$ and its denominator
is exactly $S_{xx}$. Substituting,

$$
\boxed{\;\hat\beta_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}
= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\bar x)^2}.\;}
$$ {#eq-b1}

We can state the result formally.

::: {#thm-ols}
**Theorem (OLS line, one predictor).** If $S_{xx} = \sum (x_i-\bar x)^2 > 0$ (the $x_i$
are not all identical), then $S(\beta_0,\beta_1)$ in @eq-objective has a unique minimizer

$$
\hat\beta_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \qquad \hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x ,
$$

and the fitted line $\hat y = \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 x$ passes through $(\bar x,\bar y)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Existence and the formulas are the derivation of @sec-objective through @sec-clean-slope:
the partial derivatives @eq-dSdb0–@eq-dSdb1 set to zero give the normal equations
@eq-normal, whose solution is @eq-b0 and @eq-b1. Uniqueness and the fact that the
stationary point is a *minimum* (not a maximum or saddle) are confirmed by the
second-order test in @sec-second-order, which needs $S_{xx}>0$. The point
$(\bar x,\bar y)$ lies on the line by @eq-b0, shown in @sec-through-means.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Now read @eq-b1 for what it *means*, which is the payoff of the Core box. Divide top and
bottom by $n-1$ (legal, since it cancels):

$$
\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i-\bar x)^2}
= \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(x,y)}{\operatorname{Var}(x)} .
$$ {#eq-b1-cov}

The top is the **sample covariance** of $x$ and $y$ — how much they move together — and
the bottom is the **sample variance** of $x$ — how much $x$ moves on its own. The slope
is *co-movement normalized by $x$'s own spread*. (If covariance and variance are rusty,
they are built from the ground up in [REGR-2]; here we only need that these named
formulas are what those words mean.)

This single line of reading answers a great deal at once:

- **Sign.** The slope has the sign of the covariance: positive if $x$ and $y$ tend to
  rise together, negative if one rises as the other falls, zero if they are uncorrelated.
- **Why divide by $S_{xx}$.** Covariance has units of $x\cdot y$; dividing by $x$'s
  variance (units $x^2$) leaves units of $y$ per unit $x$ — exactly a slope. The division
  also *rescales*: if $x$ barely varies ($S_{xx}$ tiny), the same co-movement implies a
  huge, fragile slope. That fragility is a real failure mode (@sec-failure).
- **What can go wrong.** If $S_{xx}=0$ — every $x_i$ identical — the denominator is zero
  and there is no slope to speak of. Geometrically all the data sit on one vertical line
  and no non-vertical line is "best." This is the boundary condition in @thm-ols, and we
  return to it in @sec-failure.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What's the relationship between the OLS slope and the correlation coefficient $r$?"* They
differ only by a ratio of standard deviations. Since $r =
S_{xy}/\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}$, a little algebra gives $\hat\beta_1 = r\cdot
\frac{s_y}{s_x}$ where $s_x,s_y$ are the sample standard deviations. So the slope is the
correlation, rescaled from the dimensionless $[-1,1]$ world of $r$ back into the units of
the data. If you first standardize both variables to unit variance, the slope *is* the
correlation. This connection is developed in [REGR-3].
:::

## The line passes through the means {#sec-through-means}

We promised this in the Core, and @eq-b0 already delivers it, but it deserves its own
moment because it is the fact most worth carrying in your head. Plug $x=\bar x$ into the
fitted line and use @eq-b0:

$$
\hat y\big|_{x=\bar x} = \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1\bar x
= (\bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x) + \hat\beta_1\bar x
= \bar y .
$$

So $(\bar x, \bar y)$ is always on the line. Intuitively: the least-squares line is a
*balanced* line — it pivots about the center of mass of the data. Tilt it however the
slope demands, but it must pass through the centroid. This is why, in practice, you can
*center* your data (subtract the means) without changing the slope at all; it only sends
the intercept to zero. @fig-pivot shows the line pivoting about $(\bar x,\bar y)$ as the
slope changes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pivot
#| fig-cap: "Every candidate line that respects the intercept relation $\\beta_0 = \\bar y - \\beta_1\\bar x$ pivots about the centroid $(\\bar x,\\bar y)$ (green dot). Changing the slope rotates the line about that fixed point. The OLS line (red) is the one rotation that minimizes total squared error; the grey lines are wrong slopes shown for contrast."
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
x = np.linspace(2, 12, 14)
y = 1.5 + 0.9 * x + rng.normal(0, 1.8, size=x.size)
xbar, ybar = x.mean(), y.mean()
b0, b1, *_ = ols_fit(x, y)
xs = np.linspace(0, 14, 100)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for slope in (0.3, 0.6, 1.3, 1.6):
    inter = ybar - slope * xbar
    ax.plot(xs, inter + slope * xs, color=GREY, lw=1.0, alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color=LINE, label=f"OLS line (slope {b1:.2f})")
ax.scatter(x, y, color=POINT, s=35, zorder=3, label="data")
ax.scatter([xbar], [ybar], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=4,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.5, label=r"centroid $(\bar x,\bar y)$")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title("All balanced lines pivot through the centroid")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Confirming it is a minimum (the second-order check) {#sec-second-order}

We claimed the stationary point is the bottom of a bowl. In Calc 1 you confirm a critical
point is a minimum with the second-derivative test: $f''>0$ means concave up. For two
variables the analogue needs three second derivatives, which we compute directly (each is
again just "differentiate, holding the other constant"). Differentiate @eq-dSdb0 and
@eq-dSdb1 once more:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial\beta_0^2}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta_0}\!\left[-2\sum (y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1 x_i)\right]
= -2\sum(-1) = 2n,
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial\beta_1^2}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta_1}\!\left[-2\sum x_i(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1 x_i)\right]
= -2\sum x_i(-x_i) = 2\sum x_i^2,
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial\beta_0\partial\beta_1}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta_1}\!\left[-2\sum (y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1 x_i)\right]
= -2\sum(-x_i) = 2\sum x_i .
$$

The first two are *positive* ($n>0$ and $\sum x_i^2 > 0$ as long as the $x$'s aren't all
zero) — the surface curves upward along each axis. The two-variable second-derivative
test asks one more thing: that the determinant
$\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial\beta_0^2}\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial\beta_1^2}
- \bigl(\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial\beta_0\partial\beta_1}\bigr)^2$ be positive. Plugging
in,

$$
(2n)\bigl(2\textstyle\sum x_i^2\bigr) - \bigl(2\sum x_i\bigr)^2
= 4\Bigl(n\sum x_i^2 - \bigl(\sum x_i\bigr)^2\Bigr)
= 4n\sum (x_i-\bar x)^2 = 4n\,S_{xx} .
$$

(The middle step uses the identity $n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 = n\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2$,
which is the second identity of @lem-identities multiplied through by $n$.) This is
**positive exactly when $S_{xx}>0$** — the same condition as in @thm-ols. So whenever the
$x$'s are not all identical, the critical point is a genuine, unique minimum. When
$S_{xx}=0$ the test fails and, consistently, the slope formula @eq-b1 divides by zero:
there is simply no best line. The intuition (a convex bowl) and the algebra agree.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the formulas on real, messy data — never a toy rigged to flatter the method.
The first example we carry through *by hand, number by number*, so you watch the formulas
breathe.

## Example 1 — Old Faithful: predicting eruption length from waiting time {#sec-faithful}

A classic, genuinely linear dataset: the Old Faithful geyser. Each observation pairs the
*waiting time* until an eruption (minutes) with the *duration* of that eruption (minutes).
Naturalists want to predict how long the next eruption will last from how long visitors
have been waiting. We take a small, representative slice of $n=10$ observations so we can
do every arithmetic step in the open.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-faithful-data
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
wait = np.array([56, 57, 62, 68, 71, 73, 79, 81, 84, 89], dtype=float)
dur  = np.array([1.9, 2.1, 2.4, 3.6, 3.8, 3.9, 4.3, 4.1, 4.6, 4.9])
n = len(wait)
xbar, ybar = wait.mean(), dur.mean()
Sxx = np.sum((wait - xbar) ** 2)
Sxy = np.sum((wait - xbar) * (dur - ybar))
b1 = Sxy / Sxx
b0 = ybar - b1 * xbar
print(f"n          = {n}")
print(f"sum x      = {wait.sum():.1f}    xbar = {xbar:.3f}")
print(f"sum y      = {dur.sum():.2f}     ybar = {ybar:.3f}")
print(f"S_xx       = sum (x - xbar)^2          = {Sxx:.3f}")
print(f"S_xy       = sum (x - xbar)(y - ybar)  = {Sxy:.3f}")
print(f"beta1_hat  = S_xy / S_xx               = {b1:.4f}")
print(f"beta0_hat  = ybar - beta1*xbar         = {b0:.4f}")
```

Walk the arithmetic the code just printed, plugging straight into the boxed formulas:

- The means are $\bar x = `{python} f"{xbar:.1f}"`$ minutes of waiting and
  $\bar y = `{python} f"{ybar:.2f}"`$ minutes of eruption.
- The spread of $x$ about its mean is $S_{xx} = `{python} f"{Sxx:.1f}"`$, and the
  co-movement of $x$ and $y$ is $S_{xy} = `{python} f"{Sxy:.2f}"`$. Both are positive:
  longer waits go with longer eruptions.
- The slope is $\hat\beta_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx} = `{python} f"{Sxy:.2f}"` /
  `{python} f"{Sxx:.1f}"` = `{python} f"{b1:.4f}"`$. Reading the units: each extra minute
  of waiting buys about $`{python} f"{b1:.3f}"`$ extra minutes of eruption.
- The intercept is $\hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x = `{python} f"{ybar:.2f}"` -
  (`{python} f"{b1:.4f}"`)(`{python} f"{xbar:.1f}"`) = `{python} f"{b0:.3f}"`$.

So the fitted line is
$\widehat{\text{duration}} = `{python} f"{b0:.2f}"` + `{python} f"{b1:.3f}"`\,\text{wait}$.
A sanity check against @sec-through-means: at $x=\bar x = `{python} f"{xbar:.1f}"`$ the
line predicts $`{python} f"{b0 + b1*xbar:.2f}"`$, which is $\bar y$ to rounding — the line
goes through the centroid, as it must. @fig-faithful draws the fit and its residuals.

```{python}
#| label: fig-faithful
#| fig-cap: "Old Faithful, $n=10$. **Left:** the fitted OLS line through the data, passing through the centroid (green). **Right:** the residuals $e_i = y_i - \\hat y_i$ plotted against $x$. They scatter around zero with no obvious pattern — the linear model is appropriate here. Their *signed* sum is zero (first normal equation) and their $x$-weighted sum is zero (second normal equation), the visual content of @eq-normal."
fitted = b0 + b1 * wait
resid = dur - fitted
xs = np.linspace(wait.min() - 3, wait.max() + 3, 100)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))
axL.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color=LINE, label=f"$\\hat y = {b0:.2f} + {b1:.3f}x$")
for xi, yi, fi in zip(wait, dur, fitted):
    axL.plot([xi, xi], [yi, fi], color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axL.scatter(wait, dur, color=POINT, s=40, zorder=3, label="data")
axL.scatter([xbar], [ybar], color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=4, edgecolor="white", label="centroid")
axL.set_xlabel("waiting time (min)"); axL.set_ylabel("eruption duration (min)")
axL.set_title("Fitted line"); axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(wait, resid, color=POINT, s=40, zorder=3)
for xi, ri in zip(wait, resid):
    axR.plot([xi, xi], [0, ri], color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.set_xlabel("waiting time (min)"); axR.set_ylabel("residual $e_i$")
axR.set_title("Residuals vs. $x$ (no pattern = good)")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"check: sum of residuals      = {resid.sum(): .3e}  (≈ 0, first normal eqn)")
print(f"check: sum of x_i * residual = {np.sum(wait*resid): .3e}  (≈ 0, second normal eqn)")
```

The printed checks confirm the derivation operationally: the residuals sum to zero and
their $x$-weighted sum is zero, the two normal equations made visible. The residual plot
on the right is your first diagnostic tool — a structureless cloud around zero is what a
good simple-regression fit looks like. Structure in that plot (a curve, a fan) is the
signal that an assumption is breaking, the subject of [REGR-2] and [REGR-10].

## Example 2 — a near-linear economic relationship with real noise {#sec-mortgage}

A second honest example, with more points and more scatter, so you see the method working
where the relationship is real but loose. We relate a regional **median household income**
(in thousands of dollars) to **average annual household spending on dining out** (also in
thousands) across $n=40$ synthetic-but-realistic localities. Spending genuinely rises with
income, but with substantial person-to-person variation — exactly the regime regression is
built for.

```{python}
#| label: fig-mortgage
#| fig-cap: "A noisier but genuinely linear relationship ($n=40$): dining-out spending vs. household income. The OLS line captures the central trend; the wide but patternless residual band on the right confirms the linear model is honest here, just imprecise for any single locality. This is the *typical* habitat of simple regression — a real signal inside real noise."
rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)
income = rng.uniform(35, 140, size=40)
spend = 0.8 + 0.045 * income + rng.normal(0, 0.9, size=income.size)
b0m, b1m, Sxxm, Sxym = ols_fit(income, spend)
fittedm = b0m + b1m * income
residm = spend - fittedm
xs = np.linspace(income.min(), income.max(), 100)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))
axL.scatter(income, spend, color=POINT, s=30, alpha=0.85, label="localities")
axL.plot(xs, b0m + b1m * xs, color=LINE, label=f"$\\hat y = {b0m:.2f} + {b1m:.3f}x$")
axL.set_xlabel("median income ($000)"); axL.set_ylabel("dining-out spend ($000)")
axL.set_title("Fitted line"); axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(income, residm, color=POINT, s=30, alpha=0.85)
axR.set_xlabel("median income ($000)"); axR.set_ylabel("residual")
axR.set_title("Residuals vs. $x$")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"slope  beta1 = {b1m:.4f}  (≈ ${b1m*1000:.0f} more dining spend per $1000 income)")
print(f"interc beta0 = {b0m:.4f}")
print(f"S_xx = {Sxxm:.1f}   S_xy = {Sxym:.2f}")
```

Same two formulas, larger data: the slope says each additional \$1,000 of median income is
associated with about \$$`{python} f"{b1m*1000:.0f}"`$ more annual dining-out spending.
The residual band is wide — the line will not predict any single locality precisely — but
it is *flat and patternless*, so the line is an honest summary of the central tendency. The
gap between "captures the trend" and "predicts an individual well" is the difference
between the slope's meaning and the residual spread, quantified in [REGR-3].

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The Core's choice — squared vertical error — has consequences, and an honest treatment
shows where the line breaks. This is the section that separates "can compute a slope" from
"knows when to trust one."

## Outliers: one point can capture the line {#sec-outliers}

Because error is *squared*, a single far-off point contributes a giant term to
@eq-objective, and the line will tilt dramatically to reduce it. @fig-outlier shows the
same clean dataset with and without one corrupted point — perhaps a data-entry error or a
genuinely anomalous case.

```{python}
#| label: fig-outlier
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. **Left:** a clean linear cloud and its OLS line. **Right:** the *same* data with one point moved to $(9, 1)$ — a single outlier. Because squared error punishes large gaps quadratically, that one point levers the entire line away from the rest (dashed grey = original fit, red = fit with the outlier). OLS has zero robustness to outliers; this is its sharpest limitation."
rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
x = np.linspace(1, 9, 12)
y = 1.0 + 1.0 * x + rng.normal(0, 0.7, size=x.size)

b0c, b1c, *_ = ols_fit(x, y)

x_out = x.copy(); y_out = y.copy()
y_out[-1] = 1.0          # drag the rightmost point far below the trend
x_out[-1] = 9.0
b0o, b1o, *_ = ols_fit(x_out, y_out)

xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7), sharey=True)
axL.scatter(x, y, color=POINT, s=40, zorder=3)
axL.plot(xs, b0c + b1c * xs, color=LINE, label=f"slope {b1c:.2f}")
axL.set_title("Clean data"); axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$")
axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.scatter(x_out[:-1], y_out[:-1], color=POINT, s=40, zorder=3)
axR.scatter([x_out[-1]], [y_out[-1]], color="black", s=90, marker="X", zorder=4, label="outlier")
axR.plot(xs, b0c + b1c * xs, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=f"orig slope {b1c:.2f}")
axR.plot(xs, b0o + b1o * xs, color=LINE, label=f"new slope {b1o:.2f}")
axR.set_title("One outlier captures the line"); axR.set_xlabel("$x$")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The slope falls from $`{python} f"{b1c:.2f}"`$ to $`{python} f"{b1o:.2f}"`$ on the
strength of one point. **When you reach for something else:** if outliers are a real
worry, use a robust loss — least *absolute* deviations (the median-style fit hinted at in
@sec-why), or Huber/Tukey M-estimators that grow linearly rather than quadratically for
large residuals. Diagnosing *which* points are doing the levering is the job of [REGR-10
*Diagnostics, Leverage, and Influence*].

## Nonlinearity: a perfectly computed line for a curved truth {#sec-nonlinear}

The formulas always return *a* line; they cannot tell you the relationship was never
linear. @fig-nonlinear fits OLS to data that genuinely curve. The slope and intercept are
computed flawlessly, and they are nearly useless — the residual plot screams the problem.

```{python}
#| label: fig-nonlinear
#| fig-cap: "OLS applied where it does not belong. The truth is a curve ($y$ rises then falls), but OLS dutifully returns a straight line (left). The tell is the residual plot (right): a clear $\\cap$-shaped pattern, not a flat cloud. The residuals are not random noise — they carry the curvature the line could not. *A patterned residual plot is the universal signal that the linear form is wrong.*"
rng = np.random.default_rng(8)
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 40)
y = 2 + 4 * x - 0.42 * x ** 2 + rng.normal(0, 1.5, size=x.size)
b0n, b1n, *_ = ols_fit(x, y)
fittedn = b0n + b1n * x
residn = y - fittedn
xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))
axL.scatter(x, y, color=POINT, s=30)
axL.plot(xs, b0n + b1n * xs, color=LINE, label=f"OLS line (slope {b1n:.2f})")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$"); axL.set_title("Straight line, curved truth")
axL.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(x, residn, color=POINT, s=30)
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("residual"); axR.set_title("Residuals reveal the curvature")
plt.tight_layout()
```

**When you reach for something else:** if the residuals show curvature, the fix is often
still least squares — applied to *transformed* predictors (a polynomial or basis
expansion, [REGR-20]) or after transforming $y$. Truly local, form-free methods
(splines, kernels, LOESS) live at the far end of this track ([REGR-21]–[REGR-24]). The key
practitioner habit: **always plot the residuals**, because the fit summary alone will not
warn you.

## Anscombe's quartet: identical statistics, four different realities {#sec-anscombe}

The sharpest single demonstration that the slope and intercept are *not enough* is Frank
Anscombe's 1973 quartet: four datasets engineered to share, to two decimal places, the
same $\bar x$, $\bar y$, the same slope, intercept, and correlation — yet they look
nothing alike.

```{python}
#| label: fig-anscombe
#| fig-cap: "Anscombe's quartet. All four datasets yield essentially the *same* OLS line ($\\hat y \\approx 3.00 + 0.500x$) and the same correlation, yet only the first is a genuine linear relationship. Dataset II is a clean curve; III is an exact line dragged by one outlier; IV is a vertical stack whose slope hangs entirely on a single far-right point. The numbers are blind to all of this — the plot is not."
# Anscombe's actual published quartet.
ax_data = {
 "I":  (np.array([10,8,13,9,11,14,6,4,12,7,5.]),
        np.array([8.04,6.95,7.58,8.81,8.33,9.96,7.24,4.26,10.84,4.82,5.68])),
 "II": (np.array([10,8,13,9,11,14,6,4,12,7,5.]),
        np.array([9.14,8.14,8.74,8.77,9.26,8.10,6.13,3.10,9.13,7.26,4.74])),
 "III":(np.array([10,8,13,9,11,14,6,4,12,7,5.]),
        np.array([7.46,6.77,12.74,7.11,7.81,8.84,6.08,5.39,8.15,6.42,5.73])),
 "IV": (np.array([8,8,8,8,8,8,8,19,8,8,8.]),
        np.array([6.58,5.76,7.71,8.84,8.47,7.04,5.25,12.50,5.56,7.91,6.89])),
}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8.4, 6.2))
xs = np.linspace(2, 20, 100)
for ax, (name, (xx, yy)) in zip(axes.ravel(), ax_data.items()):
    bb0, bb1, *_ = ols_fit(xx, yy)
    ax.scatter(xx, yy, color=POINT, s=35, zorder=3)
    ax.plot(xs, bb0 + bb1 * xs, color=LINE)
    ax.set_title(f"Dataset {name}:  $\\hat y = {bb0:.2f} + {bb1:.3f}x$", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(2, 20); ax.set_ylim(2, 14)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never trust the slope and intercept without looking at the data and the residuals.**
Anscombe's quartet is the permanent reminder: identical regression output can hide a clean
line, a curve, an outlier-driven artifact, or a near-degenerate $x$. The summary numbers
are a *compression* of the data and, like any lossy compression, they discard exactly the
information — curvature, outliers, leverage — that decides whether the line means anything.
The figure is not optional decoration; it is part of the analysis.
:::

## The degenerate boundary: no spread in $x$ {#sec-degenerate}

Finally, the boundary baked into @thm-ols. If every $x_i$ is the same value, then
$S_{xx} = \sum (x_i-\bar x)^2 = 0$, the slope @eq-b1 divides by zero, and the
second-order determinant $4n S_{xx}$ is zero — the bowl degenerates into a trough with no
unique bottom. This is not a numerical glitch; it is the honest statement that **you
cannot learn how $y$ changes with $x$ if $x$ never changed.** Near-degeneracy (tiny but
nonzero $S_{xx}$) is just as dangerous: the slope becomes wildly sensitive, the
one-predictor shadow of *multicollinearity*, which gets its full treatment in [REGR-7].

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is simple OLS the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"* The honest summary you
should be able to recite:

- **Use it** when (a) you have one quantitative predictor and a quantitative outcome, (b) a
  scatterplot looks plausibly linear, (c) the residual plot is a flat, patternless cloud,
  and (d) no single point dominates. In that regime it is unbeatable: closed-form,
  interpretable, fast, and — as [REGR-6] proves — the best linear unbiased estimator.
- **Do not use it** when the relationship is curved (transform or go nonparametric —
  [REGR-20]+), when outliers dominate (robust regression), when the error spread changes
  across $x$ (weighted least squares, [REGR-11]), or when $x$ barely varies (degenerate).
- **And remember the deepest caveat:** the line estimates *association*, not causation. A
  positive slope means $y$ tends to be higher where $x$ is higher in this data — nothing
  about whether changing $x$ would change $y$.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. In practice you would *not*
type out the sums. Every numerical stack has it: `numpy.polyfit(x, y, 1)` or a
`scipy.stats.linregress(x, y)` (which also returns the correlation and standard errors),
or `statsmodels`/`sklearn` for the full inferential apparatus. Internally these solve the
normal equations @eq-normal — though for numerical stability they use a QR or SVD
factorization of the design matrix rather than forming $\sum x_i^2$ directly, because the
raw sums can lose precision when the $x$'s are large. But the *answer* they return is
exactly $\hat\beta_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}$ and $\hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1\bar x$ — the
two boxed formulas of @sec-clean-slope. You now know what the library is doing and why.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[REGR-0 *Why a Line?*]**, which posed the regression question and
argued that "best" needs defining. This article made the definition (squared error) and
derived the unique line it produces. From here the trunk continues:

- **[REGR-2 *What the Line Assumes*]** — we have *a* line; under what conditions on the
  errors is it a *trustworthy* one? Introduces the Gauss–Markov conditions and builds the
  covariance/variance machinery we leaned on in @eq-b1-cov.
- **[REGR-3 *How Good Is the Fit?*]** — having a line, how do we *score* it? $R^2$,
  residual analysis, RMSE; the honest limits of those numbers (Anscombe, again).
- **[REGR-4 *Regression as Projection*]** — the same answer, seen as geometry: the
  residual is *orthogonal* to the predictors (the real meaning of "normal" equations,
  @sec-normal-eqs), and the fit is a projection. This generalizes effortlessly to many
  predictors.
- **[REGR-5 *Multiple Regression*]** — many predictors at once; the two normal equations
  @eq-normal become the single matrix equation $X^\top X\hat\beta = X^\top y$, with this
  article as its $p=1$ special case.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Legendre's 1805 *Nouvelles méthodes pour la détermination des
  orbites des comètes* (the first published least squares) and Gauss's 1809 *Theoria
  Motus*, which connected least squares to the normal distribution of errors — the seed of
  the maximum-likelihood view in [REGR-12].
- **The honest-statistics classic.** F. J. Anscombe, "Graphs in Statistical Analysis,"
  *The American Statistician* 27 (1973): the quartet of @fig-anscombe and the case for
  always plotting.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How uncertain are
  $\hat\beta_0,\hat\beta_1$?* — they are random, since the data are one sample; their
  sampling distribution is [REGR-6]. *Is squared error "optimal" in any provable sense?* —
  yes, the Gauss–Markov theorem, also [REGR-6]. *What if I want the line that minimizes
  perpendicular distance, not vertical?* — that is total least squares / principal
  components, a different objective that lives in the Geometry-of-Data track.
